diagrams.title: Binary Tree for Traversal Examples
diagrams.description: A binary tree with root 50, left child 30, right child 70, where 30 has children 20 and 40, and 70 has children 60 and 80
diagrams.purpose: Use this tree to practice all four traversal methods and verify outputs: Preorder: 50,30,20,40,70,60,80 | Inorder: 20,30,40,50,60,70,80 | Postorder: 20,40,30,60,80,70,50 | Level Order: 50,30,70,20,40,60,80

diagrams.title: Recursion Stack Visualization for Inorder Traversal
diagrams.description: Shows how recursion stack grows and shrinks while traversing a sample tree, demonstrating the call stack depth at each step
elements: Root node at bottom of stack
Each recursive call adds frame to stack
Maximum stack depth equals tree height
diagrams.purpose: Understanding recursion depth and space complexity O(h). Visualize how function calls are pushed and popped during traversal.

diagrams.title: Queue Operations in Level Order Traversal
diagrams.description: Demonstrates queue state at each step of level order traversal showing enqueue and dequeue operations
elements: After processing root: enqueue left and right
diagrams.purpose: Understand FIFO behavior and how level order processes nodes breadth-first. Visualize why queue size equals maximum width at any level.

diagrams.title: Threaded Binary Tree Structure
diagrams.description: Shows a binary tree converted to threaded form with threads replacing NULL pointers
elements: Left NULL pointers thread to inorder predecessor
Right NULL pointers thread to inorder successor
diagrams.purpose: Understand how threads replace NULL links and enable traversal without stack. See how successor/predecessor links work.
